policy_statement: TMG will improve water-disaster information provision by adding flood forecast/water level information rivers, strengthening the Flood Control Integrated Information System, starting operation of the Storm Surge Disaster Prevention Information System, and continuing Tokyo Amesh rainfall information.
mechanism_description: TMG will strengthen and operate systems that provide flood, water-level, storm-surge, and rainfall information to improve preparedness and response for water-related disasters.
primary_causal_pathway: Climate hazard (flooding/storm surge/extreme rainfall) → improved monitoring/forecasting & warning → earlier action/response → ↓ exposure & vulnerability → ↓ climate damages
causal_mechanism_detail:
1) Change in human activity/system: Deploy, strengthen, and operate hydrometeorological monitoring, forecasting, and public information/alert systems (e.g., rainfall radar, river gauge networks, storm-surge models, integrated dissemination platforms) and associated response protocols.  
2) Mediating physical/process pathway: Improved detection and prediction of hazardous hydrological conditions increases lead time and decision quality for evacuations, protective actions (e.g., temporary barriers, pump operation), and emergency management.  
3) Climate-relevant outcome: Reduced human and economic losses from climate-related water disasters (especially as heavy precipitation and coastal surge risks intensify), i.e., increased resilience and reduced disaster impacts.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reducing flood impacts (loss of life, damage, and disruption) by improving early warning and emergency response to extreme rainfall, river flooding, and storm surge.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: The mechanism acts on disaster preparedness/response—turning climate hazard information into earlier action—thereby reducing exposure and vulnerability to floods and storm surge.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While information systems may have minor co-benefits (e.g., operational efficiency), they do not primarily reduce energy use/GHGs (Mitigation) nor primarily optimize resource inputs (Resource Efficiency) nor enhance ecosystems (Nature-Based Solutions).  
3) Edge cases applied: If an information system were explicitly used mainly to optimize water/energy operations for emissions reductions, classification could shift, but that is not the typical or dominant purpose of flood/storm-surge warning systems.  
4) Applicability to ALL instances: Any policy under this canonical mechanism—flood/water-level/storm-surge/rainfall information provision aimed at preparedness and response—has its main climate effect through reduced disaster risk and impacts, i.e., Adaptation.  
5) Secondary subtype note: More specific adaptation subtype (e.g., early warning systems, emergency management, climate services) should be assigned based on policy-level evidence and is deferred.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: If deployed primarily for real-time control that reduces emissions (e.g., optimizing pump energy with explicit GHG targets), there could be a mitigation/resource-efficiency secondary effect; however, the canonical mechanism as stated is clearly centered on preparedness/response and impact reduction.
key_indicators: "specifying additional flood forecast rivers"; "water level information rivers"; "strengthening the Flood Control Integrated Information System"; "starting the operation of the Storm Surge Disaster Prevention Information System"; "provide rainfall information through Tokyo Amesh"
instance_edge_case_notes: Could be interpreted as an information-disclosure instrument, but the dominant action is system strengthening/operation (IT and monitoring infrastructure), so infrastructure-investment is the best fit.
